citations.citation_title: Standards of Care in Diabetes - Obesity and Weight Management for the Prevention and Treatment of Type 2 Diabetes
citations.guideline_source: American Diabetes Association (ADA) 2024 Standards of Care in Diabetes
citations.quote: In adults with type 2 diabetes and overweight or obesity, GLP-1 receptor agonists with greater weight loss efficacy (e.g., semaglutide, tirzepatide) are preferred pharmacotherapy options to support both glycemic and weight management goals.
citations.supports_claim: Semaglutide (Ozempic) is a guideline-preferred therapy for patients with type 2 diabetes and obesity (BMI >= 30), supporting medical necessity.

citations.citation_title: AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults
citations.guideline_source: American Heart Association / American College of Cardiology / The Obesity Society 2013 Guideline
citations.quote: Adults with a BMI of 30 or greater, or BMI of 27 or greater with weight-related comorbidities, should be offered comprehensive lifestyle intervention and may be candidates for adjunctive pharmacotherapy to achieve and sustain weight loss.
citations.supports_claim: BMI >= 30 qualifies the patient for pharmacologic weight-loss therapy in addition to lifestyle intervention.

citations.citation_title: AACE/ACE Comprehensive Clinical Practice Guidelines for Medical Care of Patients with Obesity
citations.guideline_source: American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists / American College of Endocrinology 2016 Obesity Guidelines
citations.quote: Pharmacotherapy for obesity is recommended as an adjunct to lifestyle intervention for patients with BMI >= 30, or BMI >= 27 with complications, when lifestyle therapy alone has been insufficient to achieve weight-loss goals.
citations.supports_claim: GLP-1 receptor agonist pharmacotherapy is medically appropriate for a patient with BMI 34 after lifestyle intervention.

citations.citation_title: Pharmacological Management of Obesity: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline
citations.quote: Weight-loss pharmacotherapy is recommended for patients with a BMI of 30 kg/m2 or greater, or a BMI of 27 kg/m2 or greater with at least one weight-related comorbidity, who have been unable to achieve or sustain clinically meaningful weight loss with a structured lifestyle intervention alone, as an adjunct to ongoing diet, exercise, and behavior modification.
citations.supports_claim: BMI ≥ 30 with inadequate response to lifestyle intervention qualifies the patient for weight-loss pharmacotherapy such as a GLP-1 receptor agonist.

citations.citation_title: 2013 AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults
citations.guideline_source: American Heart Association / American College of Cardiology / The Obesity Society 2013
citations.quote: Adults with a BMI of 30 kg/m2 or greater, or a BMI of 27 kg/m2 or greater with weight-related comorbidities such as hypertension or type 2 diabetes mellitus, should be offered comprehensive lifestyle interventions, and pharmacotherapy is an appropriate adjunct in these BMI categories when lifestyle measures alone do not achieve sufficient weight loss.
citations.supports_claim: Patient with BMI 34 meets the BMI ≥ 30 threshold at which pharmacotherapy is an appropriate adjunct to lifestyle intervention.

citations.citation_title: AACE/ACE Comprehensive Clinical Practice Guidelines for Medical Care of Patients with Obesity
citations.guideline_source: American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists / American College of Endocrinology 2016
citations.quote: Pharmacotherapy may be initiated concurrently with comprehensive lifestyle intervention in patients with obesity-related complications, rather than being reserved exclusively for patients who have failed a trial of lifestyle change in isolation.
citations.supports_claim: A rigid 6-month documented lifestyle-failure prerequisite is not consistent with guideline practice; pharmacotherapy may be started concurrently with lifestyle intervention.
